article.title: StWRKY65 stimulates thermotolerance in potato (Solanum tuberosum L.) through antioxidant and photosynthetic modulation

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression increases activities of APX, CAT, POD and SOD
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p063.s001: This research highlights the essential function of StWRKY65, a Group II WRKY transcription factor localized in the nucleus, in improving thermotolerance in potato. PMC12319013.p063.s002: Overexpression of StWRKY65 markedly improves thermotolerance by enhancing the activities of key antioxidant enzymes (APX, CAT, POD, SOD), increasing proline levels, maintain chlorophyll content, optimizing photosynthetic parameters (including photosynthesis, transpiration, and stomatal conductance), and decreasing H2O2 and MDA accumulation. PMC12319013.p063.s003: Additionally, the overexpression of StWRKY65 was shown to transcriptionally activate important genes associated with antioxidant enzymes, including StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2. PMC12319013.p063.s004: In contrast, plants with StWRKY65 knockdown show reduced thermotolerance and adverse physiological responses.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 is expressed in potato leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p032.s001: This study systematically analyzed the expression profiles of the StWRKY65 gene in leaves of two potato cultivars (‘Desiree’ and ‘Atlantic’) under various abiotic stress conditions ( Figures 2A–L ). PMC12319013.p032.s002: Under drought stress, StWRKY65 exhibited expression patterns correlated with stress severity and cultivar specificity. PMC12319013.p032.s003: In ‘Desiree’, 10% PEG6000 treatment induced a stable upregulation (with relatively steady increases at 1, 3, 6, 12, and 48 h post-treatment, peaking at 6 h), whereas 20% PEG6000 triggered fluctuating upregulation (rapid induction at 1 h, followed by a decline at 3 h, then gradual increases at 6, 12, and 24 h, reaching a peak at 24 h) ( Figures 2A–C ), suggesting its positive regulatory role under severe drought. PMC12319013.p032.s004: In ‘Atlantic’, 10% PEG6000 elicited oscillatory expression (upregulation at 1, 3, 24, and 48 h but downregulation at 6 and 12 h), while 20% PEG6000 induced variable upregulation (peaking at 12 h) ( Figures 2B–D ), indicating its potential involvement in drought resistance.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 localizes to nucleus
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p037.s003: Confocal laser scanning microscopy analysis revealed distinct subcellular distribution patterns: The free GFP control exhibited fluorescence in the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus, indicating its nonspecific cellular localization. PMC12319013.p037.s004: In contrast, the StWRKY65-GFP fusion protein displayed strong green fluorescence signals exclusively in the nuclear region, with no detectable fluorescence in the cytoplasm or plasma membrane ( Figure 3 ). PMC12319013.p037.s005: This strict nuclear localization pattern confirms that StWRKY65 specifically resides in the nucleus, a key characteristic of functional TFs involved in transcriptional regulation. PMC12319013.p037.s006: These findings provide crucial structural evidence supporting the presumed role of StWRKY65 in regulating gene expression networks within the nucleus, laying a foundation for further mechanistic studies on its biological functions.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression increases plant height, tuber yield and biomass
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p058.s003: Our study investigated the role of the StWRKY65 gene in enhancing thermotolerance in potato plants under heat stress (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p058.s004: Remarkably, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited a significant increase in plant height, tuber yield, total fresh and dry biomass, and root systems compared to NT controls, as recorded in Figures 4 , 5A–F . PMC12319013.p058.s005: Conversely, RNAi-silenced plants showed a decline in these growth parameters, confirming the gene’s critical role in heat stress resilience. PMC12319013.p058.s006: Notably, under optimal temperatures, all experimental plants performed equally, supporting that StWRKY65 specifically functions in thermotolerance rather than baseline growth. PMC12319013.p058.s007: In a previous report, after mannitol (200–300 mM) stress application, lateral root number remained unchanged, but root length decreased in WT and OE lines.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 upregulates antioxidant genes under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s009: Our findings under heat stress showed similar research trend to a previous research under cold and drought stress, which described that the overexpression of the Malus baccata WRKY TF, MbWRKY65 increased drought and freezing tolerance in transgenic tomatoes by reducing oxidative stress markers (MDA, H2O2, O2 -) and enhancing proline, chlorophyll, and antioxidant enzyme activity. PMC12319013.p059.s010: Additionally, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes (StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2) under heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p059.s011: Conversely, RNAi-mediated suppression of StWRKY65 resulted in downregulation of these genes compared to NT controls ( Figures 8A–T ). PMC12319013.p059.s012: These findings suggest that StWRKY65 plays a pivotal role in modulating antioxidant-related gene expression during heat stress. PMC12319013.p059.s013: A prior study exhibited the functional analysis of TaWRKY31, which revealed contrasting expression patterns of antioxidant genes under drought stress conditions.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 modulates growth and biomass accumulation under heat stress

events.event_label: StWRKY65 localizes to the nucleus
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s009: As TFs, WRKY proteins are predominantly localized in the nucleus and exhibit transcriptional regulatory activity. PMC12319013.p057.s010: Subcellular localization analysis demonstrated nuclear localization of the StWRKY65 protein, as evidenced by distinct GFP fluorescence signals exclusively detected in the nucleus ( Figure 3 ). PMC12319013.p057.s011: In contrast, control vectors expressing free GFP exhibited diffuse fluorescence patterns distributed across the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nuclear compartments. PMC12319013.p057.s012: This observation confirms the nuclear targeting capability of StWRKY65, consistent with its predicted function as a TF. PMC12319013.p057.s013: The BrWRKY65-GFP fusion protein was transiently expressed in Nicotiana benthamiana epidermal cells.

events.event_label: GFP localizes to plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p037.s001: To elucidate the subcellular localization of StWRKY65, its coding sequence was cloned into the pCAM35S expression vector carrying the green fluorescent protein (GFP) reporter gene. PMC12319013.p037.s002: The constructed StWRKY65-GFP fusion vector, along with the empty GFP vector (control), was transiently expressed in epidermal cells of Nicotiana benthamiana via Agrobacterium tumefaciens (strain GV3101)-mediated transformation. PMC12319013.p037.s003: Confocal laser scanning microscopy analysis revealed distinct subcellular distribution patterns: The free GFP control exhibited fluorescence in the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus, indicating its nonspecific cellular localization. PMC12319013.p037.s004: In contrast, the StWRKY65-GFP fusion protein displayed strong green fluorescence signals exclusively in the nuclear region, with no detectable fluorescence in the cytoplasm or plasma membrane ( Figure 3 ).

events.event_label: StWRKY65 affects plant height, fresh weight, dry weight, root fresh weight, root dry weight, and tuber yield per plant under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p043.s001: StWRKY65 modulates potato growth parameters of cultivar ‘Atlantic’; (A) plant height, (B) fresh weight, (C) dry weight, (D) root fresh weight, (E) root dry weight, and (F) tuber yield per plant, after exposure to 20°C, 30°C, and 35°C of heat stress treatments. PMC12319013.p043.s002: NT, non-transgenic plants; OE, pBI121-EGFP-StWRKY65-transgenic plants (OE-1, OE-2, and OE-4); RNAi, pART-StWRKY65-RNAi-transgenic plants (RNAi-1, RNAi-3 and RNAi-4). PMC12319013.p043.s003: The data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. PMC12319013.p043.s004: P-values (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01) were calculated through ordinary two-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s multiple comparisons test with a sample size of n = 9.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 upregulates antioxidant genes under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p050.s001: To further elucidate the role of StWRKY65 in response to varying temperature stress conditions (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C), we analyzed the expression of stress-responsive genes in two potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’), including StAPX, StCAT1,​StCAT2, tPOD12, StPOD47 StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3,​StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2. PMC12319013.p050.s002: As shown in Figures 8A–T , under 20°C, no significant differences in the relative mRNA expression levels of these antioxidant-related genes were observed between transgenic (OE and RNAi) and NT plants in either cultivar. PMC12319013.p050.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, the StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited​significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes, including StAPX ( Figures 8A, B ), StCAT1 ( Figures 8C, D ) StCAT2 ( Figures 8E, F ), StPOD12 ( Figures 8G, H ), StPOD47 ( Figures 8I, J ), StFeSOD2 ( Figures 8K, L ), StFeSOD3 ( Figures 8M, N ), StMnSOD ( Figures 8O, P ), StCuZnSOD1 ( Figures 8Q, R ), and StCuZnSOD2 ( Figures 8S, T ), compared to NT controls. PMC12319013.p050.s004: In contrast, the RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant downregulation of these genes under the same stress conditions.

events.event_label: WRKY transcription factors are present in multiple plant species
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p056.s001: WRKY TFs are widely distributed in plants, such as Arabidopsis, tobacco, rice, tomato, wheat, and maize, and play key regulatory roles in various life processes and stress responses as part of the plant stress signaling pathway. PMC12319013.p056.s002: Despite the critical role of WRKY TFs in abiotic stress responses, their functional characterization in potato, the world’s fourth most important staple crop, remains poorly understood, particularly under heat stress conditions. PMC12319013.p056.s003: Here, we investigated the function of StWRKY65 under different heat stresses through transgenic techniques to elucidate its regulatory role in thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p056.s004: In this study, we initially investigated the sequence alignment analysis, which revealed that StWRKY65 contains a canonical WRKYGQK domain at its N-terminus, along with a C2H2-type zinc-finger motif (CX5-C-X23-H-X1-H), consistent with its classification as a Group II WRKY transcription factor ( Figure 1A ).

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression enhances thermotolerance and multiple growth traits under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s006: Additionally, we generated transgenic potato lines with overexpression (OE) or RNA interference (RNAi)-mediated suppression of the target gene. PMC12319013.p002.s007: Under heat stress, OE lines exhibited enhanced thermotolerance, manifested through improvements in plant height, fresh/dry weights of plants and roots, tuber yield, photosynthetic efficiency, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance. PMC12319013.p002.s008: Concurrently, compared to non-transgenic (NT) controls, StWRKY65 overexpression significantly elevated the activities of antioxidant enzymes (ascorbate peroxidase [APX], catalase [CAT], peroxidase [POD], superoxide dismutase [SOD]), proline accumulation, and total chlorophyll content, while reducing malondialdehyde (MDA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) levels. PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p002.s010: Furthermore, StWRKY65 overexpression transcriptionally activated key antioxidant enzyme-related genes (StAPX, StCAT1/2, StPOD12/47, StFeSOD2/3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1/2), whereas its knockdown produced opposing effects.

PMC12319013.p048.s004: P-values (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001) were calculated through ordinary two-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s multiple comparisons test with a sample size of n = 9.

events.event_label: Heat stress enhances activities of APX, CAT, POD, and SOD in StWRKY65 overexpression lines
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p046.s001: To investigate the physiological responses of transgenic and NT potato lines of the cultivars ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ under different temperature stress conditions (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C), the key antioxidant enzymes (APX, CAT, POD, SOD), oxidative stress marker (H2O2 and MDA), osmoprotectant proline, and total chlorophyll content were compared between NT and transgenic lines (OE and RNAi), as shown in Figures 6 , 7 . PMC12319013.p046.s002: Under the control condition (20°C), no significant differences (P > 0.05) were observed in all the aforementioned physiological indicators between transgenic and NT plants. PMC12319013.p046.s003: Under the conditions of 30°C and 35°C, the activities of APX ( Figures 6 , 7A ), CAT ( Figures 6 , 7B ), POD ( Figures 6 , 7C ), and SOD ( Figures 6 , 7D ) in the OE lines were significantly higher than those in the NT and RNAi lines (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001), indicating that the overexpression of StWRKY65 could enhance the antioxidant defense system and more efficiently scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) under heat stress. PMC12319013.p046.s004: Meanwhile, the contents of oxidative stress markers H2O2 ( Figures 6 , 7E ) and MDA ( Figures 6 , 7F ) were also analyzed.

events.event_label: Heat stress increases plant height, fresh weight, root fresh weight, root dry weight, and tuber weight in StWRKY65 overexpression lines
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p042.s003: When subjected to 30°C heat stress, all growth indices of ‘Atlantic’ genotypes were growth-inhibited, but StWRKY65 overexpression lines exhibited stronger adaptability: compared with NT controls, plant height increased by 26.52%–33.45%, plant fresh weight by 22.86%–27.55%, dry weight by 25.38%–30.41%, root fresh weight by 24.07%–32.33%, root dry weight by 26.29%, 36.85%, and tuber weight by 35.59%–47.64%. PMC12319013.p042.s004: In contrast, RNAi lines showed decreases in plant height (26.29%–31.76%), plant fresh weight (29.62%–32.81%), dry weight (25.25%–30.45%), root fresh weight (23.9%–30.25%), root dry weight (26.18%–31.53%), and tuber weight (35.33%–49.89%) compared with NT controls ( Figures 4A–F ). PMC12319013.p042.s005: Under severe 35°C heat stress, the thermoprotective effect of StWRKY65 in ‘Atlantic’ became more pronounced: OE lines displayed remarkable stress resistance, with plant height increased by 51.10%–64.90%, plant fresh and dry weights by 49.38%–57.67% and 36.11%–36.93%, respectively, root fresh and dry weights by 46.42%–63.97% and 43.15%–57.38%, respectively, and tuber weight by 77.56%–97.53% relative to NT controls. PMC12319013.p042.s006: Conversely, RNAi lines were highly sensitive to heat stress, showing drastic reductions in plant height (51.38%–61.70%), plant fresh and dry weights (57.77%–65.16% and 50.61%–59.24%), root fresh and dry weights (46.53%–56.27% and 43.28%–57.82%), and tuber weight (92.32%–94.91%) ( Figures 4A–F ), compared to NT lines. events.event_label: StWRKY65 is temperature‑responsive and contributes to thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p034.s001: The expression profiles of StWRKY65 under high-temperature stress demonstrated robust and rapid induction ( Figures 2E–H ). PMC12319013.p034.s002: In ‘Desiree’, 30°C treatment caused differential upregulation (gradual increases at 1, 3, and 6 h, a decline at 12 h, followed by rises at 24 and 48 h, peaking at 48 h), whereas 35°C elicited stronger upregulation throughout the time course (particularly from 6 to 48 h), with a rapid response peaking at 6 h ( Figures 2E–G ). PMC12319013.p034.s003: This suggests that StWRKY65 is temperature-responsive and may play a critical role in thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p034.s004: In contrast, ‘Atlantic’ displayed distinct dynamics: at 30°C, rapid transcriptional induction occurred at 1 and 3 h, followed by declines at 6 and 12 h, then gradual increases to a peak at 48 h; under 35°C, sustained upregulation was observed at 1, 3, 6, and 12 h, a decline at 24 h, and a peak at 48 h ( Figures 2F–H ).

events.event_label: StWRKY65 confers thermotolerance and maintains photosynthetic efficiency under high temperature

events.event_label: ZmWRKY65 contains a WRKYGQK domain and a C2H2 zinc‑finger motif
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p056.s003: Here, we investigated the function of StWRKY65 under different heat stresses through transgenic techniques to elucidate its regulatory role in thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p056.s004: In this study, we initially investigated the sequence alignment analysis, which revealed that StWRKY65 contains a canonical WRKYGQK domain at its N-terminus, along with a C2H2-type zinc-finger motif (CX5-C-X23-H-X1-H), consistent with its classification as a Group II WRKY transcription factor ( Figure 1A ). PMC12319013.p056.s005: Phylogenetic tree construction further supported this classification, grouping StWRKY65 with functionally related homologs from other plant species, suggesting evolutionary conservation of its regulatory role ( Figure 1B ). PMC12319013.p056.s006: Our investigation is supported by a previous report, which explored that ZmWRKY65, a candidate WRKY TF identified from drought and SA-treated maize transcriptomic data, encodes a 516-amino acid protein featuring a conserved WRKYGQK domain, an N-terminal coiled-coil region (aa 102–142), and a C2H2 zinc-finger motif (CX5CX23 HX1H). PMC12319013.p056.s007: Phylogenetic analysis (MEGA 5.1, neighbor-joining) of 15 homologs from diverse species and 14 maize WRKYs (Phytozome 12.1) revealed close evolutionary relationships between ZmWRKY65 and AtWRKY3 (Arabidopsis), OsWRKY96 (rice), SiWRKY3 (millet), and GmWRKY33 (soybean), suggesting functional conservation among them.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 upregulates antioxidant genes
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s003: Meanwhile, oxidative markers like MDA, H2O2, and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) serve as indicators of oxidative stress levels. PMC12319013.p059.s004: A precise equilibrium between ROS generation and antioxidant activity is vital; however, heat stress can disrupt this balance, leading to excessive ROS accumulation and subsequent oxidative damage. PMC12319013.p059.s005: Furthermore, Osmolytes, such as proline, serve as key signaling molecules in enhancing plant heat stress tolerance. PMC12319013.p059.s006: Our study revealed that under heat stress (30°C and 35°C), StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants (cultivars Atlantic and Desiree) exhibited increased antioxidant enzyme activity (APX, POD, CAT, SOD), higher proline and chlorophyll levels, and reduced oxidative damage (lower MDA and H2O2) compared to NT plants ( Figures 6 , 7A–F ). PMC12319013.p059.s007: In contrast, StWRKY65 RNAi lines showed the opposite trend.

events.event_label: Heat stress reduces photosynthetic efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p060.s001: Extreme heat stress disrupts the photosynthetic apparatus in plant cells, leading to a significant decline in photosynthetic efficiency. PMC12319013.p060.s002: Our findings reveal that sustained exposure to elevated temperatures (30–35°C) induced significant photosynthetic impairment in both RNAi and NT potato plants. PMC12319013.p060.s003: This thermal stress disrupted photosynthetic performance through a cascade of interconnected physiological distresses, including a marked decline in photosynthetic efficiency, suppression of transpiration rates, and pronounced stomatal dysfunction, as evidenced by aberrant stomatal conductance and aperture regulation. PMC12319013.p060.s004: On the other hand, StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants in both cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’) under varying heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C) exhibited significantly higher photosynthetic rate, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance ( Figures 9A–F ), which maintained stomatal aperture, preserve chloroplast functions, improve water moment, and preserve ROS damage.

events.event_label: ZmWRKY65 is evolutionarily related to WRKYs from Arabidopsis, rice, millet and soybean
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p056.s006: Our investigation is supported by a previous report, which explored that ZmWRKY65, a candidate WRKY TF identified from drought and SA-treated maize transcriptomic data, encodes a 516-amino acid protein featuring a conserved WRKYGQK domain, an N-terminal coiled-coil region (aa 102–142), and a C2H2 zinc-finger motif (CX5CX23 HX1H). PMC12319013.p056.s007: Phylogenetic analysis (MEGA 5.1, neighbor-joining) of 15 homologs from diverse species and 14 maize WRKYs (Phytozome 12.1) revealed close evolutionary relationships between ZmWRKY65 and AtWRKY3 (Arabidopsis), OsWRKY96 (rice), SiWRKY3 (millet), and GmWRKY33 (soybean), suggesting functional conservation among them.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression modulates antioxidant metabolites and growth, while RNAi impairs growth under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s006: Additionally, we generated transgenic potato lines with overexpression (OE) or RNA interference (RNAi)-mediated suppression of the target gene. PMC12319013.p002.s007: Under heat stress, OE lines exhibited enhanced thermotolerance, manifested through improvements in plant height, fresh/dry weights of plants and roots, tuber yield, photosynthetic efficiency, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance. PMC12319013.p002.s008: Concurrently, compared to non-transgenic (NT) controls, StWRKY65 overexpression significantly elevated the activities of antioxidant enzymes (ascorbate peroxidase [APX], catalase [CAT], peroxidase [POD], superoxide dismutase [SOD]), proline accumulation, and total chlorophyll content, while reducing malondialdehyde (MDA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) levels. PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p002.s010: Furthermore, StWRKY65 overexpression transcriptionally activated key antioxidant enzyme-related genes (StAPX, StCAT1/2, StPOD12/47, StFeSOD2/3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1/2), whereas its knockdown produced opposing effects.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 nuclear localization
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s009: As TFs, WRKY proteins are predominantly localized in the nucleus and exhibit transcriptional regulatory activity. PMC12319013.p057.s010: Subcellular localization analysis demonstrated nuclear localization of the StWRKY65 protein, as evidenced by distinct GFP fluorescence signals exclusively detected in the nucleus ( Figure 3 ). PMC12319013.p057.s011: In contrast, control vectors expressing free GFP exhibited diffuse fluorescence patterns distributed across the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nuclear compartments. PMC12319013.p057.s012: This observation confirms the nuclear targeting capability of StWRKY65, consistent with its predicted function as a TF. PMC12319013.p057.s013: The BrWRKY65-GFP fusion protein was transiently expressed in Nicotiana benthamiana epidermal cells.

events.event_label: BrWRKY65 nuclear localization

PMC12319013.p057.s015: These results confirm BrWRKY65’s nuclear localization, consistent with our investigation.

PMC12319013.p050.s004: In contrast, the RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant downregulation of these genes under the same stress conditions. PMC12319013.p050.s005: Similarly, under severe heat stress (35°C), the OE lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) displayed even more pronounced upregulation of the aforementioned stress-responsive genes (StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2). PMC12319013.p050.s006: Conversely, compared to NT controls, the RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) exhibited significant downregulation of these antioxidant-related genes. PMC12319013.p050.s007: These findings demonstrate that StWRKY65 plays a critical role in regulating multiple antioxidant enzyme genes under heat stress (30°C and 35°C), thereby mitigating oxidative stress responses induced by elevated temperatures.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression improves thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p063.s001: This research highlights the essential function of StWRKY65, a Group II WRKY transcription factor localized in the nucleus, in improving thermotolerance in potato. PMC12319013.p063.s002: Overexpression of StWRKY65 markedly improves thermotolerance by enhancing the activities of key antioxidant enzymes (APX, CAT, POD, SOD), increasing proline levels, maintain chlorophyll content, optimizing photosynthetic parameters (including photosynthesis, transpiration, and stomatal conductance), and decreasing H2O2 and MDA accumulation. PMC12319013.p063.s004: In contrast, plants with StWRKY65 knockdown show reduced thermotolerance and adverse physiological responses.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s001: WRKY TFs typically exhibit tissue-specific expression patterns and are influenced by environmental stresses, enabling them to regulate diverse biological processes in plants. PMC12319013.p057.s003: To further elucidate the role of StWRKY65 in thermotolerance, we analyzed its expression profile in the leaf tissues of two potato cultivars, Atlantic and Desiree ( Figures 2A–L ). PMC12319013.p057.s004: qRT-PCR-based expression profiling revealed cultivar-specific differential regulation of StWRKY65 under heat stress (30°C and 35°C) at multiple time points.

events.event_label: Drought stress and PEG6000 induce StWRKY65 expression in potato leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p032.s001: This study systematically analyzed the expression profiles of the StWRKY65 gene in leaves of two potato cultivars (‘Desiree’ and ‘Atlantic’) under various abiotic stress conditions ( Figures 2A–L ). PMC12319013.p032.s002: Under drought stress, StWRKY65 exhibited expression patterns correlated with stress severity and cultivar specificity. PMC12319013.p032.s003: In ‘Desiree’, 10% PEG6000 treatment induced a stable upregulation (with relatively steady increases at 1, 3, 6, 12, and 48 h post-treatment, peaking at 6 h), whereas 20% PEG6000 triggered fluctuating upregulation (rapid induction at 1 h, followed by a decline at 3 h, then gradual increases at 6, 12, and 24 h, reaching a peak at 24 h) ( Figures 2A–C ), suggesting its positive regulatory role under severe drought. PMC12319013.p032.s004: In ‘Atlantic’, 10% PEG6000 elicited oscillatory expression (upregulation at 1, 3, 24, and 48 h but downregulation at 6 and 12 h), while 20% PEG6000 induced variable upregulation (peaking at 12 h) ( Figures 2B–D ), indicating its potential involvement in drought resistance.

PMC12319013.p034.s003: This suggests that StWRKY65 is temperature-responsive and may play a critical role in thermotolerance. events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression maintains photosynthetic efficiency under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p053.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, StWRKY65-overexpressing lines of both potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) compared to NT plants, whereas RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant decreases in these gas exchange parameters. PMC12319013.p053.s004: Similarly, under 35°C heat stress, all experimental plants of both cultivars experienced a reduction in photosynthetic efficiency, but StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) still maintained significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) than NT lines, while RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed further decreases in these photosynthetic parameters. PMC12319013.p053.s005: The contrasting responses between overexpression and knockout genotypes strongly support the regulatory role of StWRKY65 in maintaining photosynthetic efficiency under high-temperature stress (30°C and 35°C).

events.event_label: NaCl downregulates StWRKY65 expression and StWRKY65 negatively regulates salt tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p035.s003: In ‘Atlantic’, 75 mM NaCl triggered a biphasic response: initial downregulation (lowest at 1 h), followed by upregulation (peak at 3 h), then declines at 6 h and rebounds at 12 h, before significant downregulation at 24 and 48 h ( Figure 2J ). PMC12319013.p035.s004: Meanwhile, 150 mM NaCl predominantly induced downregulation (notably at 1, 3, 6, 12, and 24 h, with recovery at 48 h, lowest at 24 h) ( Figure 2L ), though the suppression was less severe than with 75 mM NaCl ( Figures 2I–L ). PMC12319013.p035.s005: These findings highlight cultivar-specific transcriptional responses of StWRKY65 to NaCl, suggesting its potential negative regulatory role in salt tolerance. PMC12319013.p035.s006: Collectively, StWRKY65 displayed markedly divergent expression patterns in ‘Desiree’ and ‘Atlantic’ across all stress conditions, underscoring how genetic differences between potato cultivars influence gene expression dynamics in response to environmental stresses.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 localizes to nucleus in epidermal cells
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p037.s001: To elucidate the subcellular localization of StWRKY65, its coding sequence was cloned into the pCAM35S expression vector carrying the green fluorescent protein (GFP) reporter gene. PMC12319013.p037.s002: The constructed StWRKY65-GFP fusion vector, along with the empty GFP vector (control), was transiently expressed in epidermal cells of Nicotiana benthamiana via Agrobacterium tumefaciens (strain GV3101)-mediated transformation. PMC12319013.p037.s003: Confocal laser scanning microscopy analysis revealed distinct subcellular distribution patterns: The free GFP control exhibited fluorescence in the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus, indicating its nonspecific cellular localization. PMC12319013.p037.s004: In contrast, the StWRKY65-GFP fusion protein displayed strong green fluorescence signals exclusively in the nuclear region, with no detectable fluorescence in the cytoplasm or plasma membrane ( Figure 3 ). PMC12319013.p037.s005: This strict nuclear localization pattern confirms that StWRKY65 specifically resides in the nucleus, a key characteristic of functional TFs involved in transcriptional regulation. PMC12319013.p037.s006: These findings provide crucial structural evidence supporting the presumed role of StWRKY65 in regulating gene expression networks within the nucleus, laying a foundation for further mechanistic studies on its biological functions.

events.event_label: High temperature perturbation alters MDA content
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p046.s003: Under the conditions of 30°C and 35°C, the activities of APX ( Figures 6 , 7A ), CAT ( Figures 6 , 7B ), POD ( Figures 6 , 7C ), and SOD ( Figures 6 , 7D ) in the OE lines were significantly higher than those in the NT and RNAi lines (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001), indicating that the overexpression of StWRKY65 could enhance the antioxidant defense system and more efficiently scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) under heat stress. PMC12319013.p046.s004: Meanwhile, the contents of oxidative stress markers H2O2 ( Figures 6 , 7E ) and MDA ( Figures 6 , 7F ) were also analyzed. PMC12319013.p046.s005: Compared with the NT lines, the OE lines had lower contents while the RNAi lines had higher contents under high temperatures (30°C and 35°C) (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001), suggesting that the OE lines suffered less oxidative damage, and the RNAi lines were more vulnerable. PMC12319013.p046.s006: In addition, the osmotic regulatory substance proline ( Figures 6 , 7G ) and total chlorophyll content ( Figures 6 , 7H ) were analyzed. PMC12319013.p046.s007: The osmoprotectant proline was significantly accumulated in the OE lines under heat stress (30°C and 35°C), which was helpful for osmotic adjustment, while the accumulation of proline in the RNAi lines was relatively less (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001).

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression upregulates antioxidant genes under 30°C heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p050.s001: To further elucidate the role of StWRKY65 in response to varying temperature stress conditions (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C), we analyzed the expression of stress-responsive genes in two potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’), including StAPX, StCAT1,​StCAT2, tPOD12, StPOD47 StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3,​StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2. PMC12319013.p050.s002: As shown in Figures 8A–T , under 20°C, no significant differences in the relative mRNA expression levels of these antioxidant-related genes were observed between transgenic (OE and RNAi) and NT plants in either cultivar. PMC12319013.p050.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, the StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited​significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes, including StAPX ( Figures 8A, B ), StCAT1 ( Figures 8C, D ) StCAT2 ( Figures 8E, F ), StPOD12 ( Figures 8G, H ), StPOD47 ( Figures 8I, J ), StFeSOD2 ( Figures 8K, L ), StFeSOD3 ( Figures 8M, N ), StMnSOD ( Figures 8O, P ), StCuZnSOD1 ( Figures 8Q, R ), and StCuZnSOD2 ( Figures 8S, T ), compared to NT controls. PMC12319013.p050.s004: In contrast, the RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant downregulation of these genes under the same stress conditions.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 regulates total fresh and dry biomass under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p058.s003: Our study investigated the role of the StWRKY65 gene in enhancing thermotolerance in potato plants under heat stress (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p058.s004: Remarkably, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited a significant increase in plant height, tuber yield, total fresh and dry biomass, and root systems compared to NT controls, as recorded in Figures 4 , 5A–F . PMC12319013.p058.s005: Conversely, RNAi-silenced plants showed a decline in these growth parameters, confirming the gene’s critical role in heat stress resilience. PMC12319013.p058.s006: Notably, under optimal temperatures, all experimental plants performed equally, supporting that StWRKY65 specifically functions in thermotolerance rather than baseline growth. PMC12319013.p058.s007: In a previous report, after mannitol (200–300 mM) stress application, lateral root number remained unchanged, but root length decreased in WT and OE lines.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 modulates thermotolerance in potato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p063.s001: This research highlights the essential function of StWRKY65, a Group II WRKY transcription factor localized in the nucleus, in improving thermotolerance in potato. PMC12319013.p063.s002: Overexpression of StWRKY65 markedly improves thermotolerance by enhancing the activities of key antioxidant enzymes (APX, CAT, POD, SOD), increasing proline levels, maintain chlorophyll content, optimizing photosynthetic parameters (including photosynthesis, transpiration, and stomatal conductance), and decreasing H2O2 and MDA accumulation. PMC12319013.p063.s003: Additionally, the overexpression of StWRKY65 was shown to transcriptionally activate important genes associated with antioxidant enzymes, including StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2. PMC12319013.p063.s004: In contrast, plants with StWRKY65 knockdown show reduced thermotolerance and adverse physiological responses. PMC12319013.p063.s005: These results position StWRKY65 as a promising target for molecular breeding aimed at enhancing heat resilience in potato and related species, thereby providing a sustainable approach to address the challenges posed by climate change on agricultural productivity.

events.event_label: BrWRKY65 nuclear localization
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s012: This observation confirms the nuclear targeting capability of StWRKY65, consistent with its predicted function as a TF. PMC12319013.p057.s013: The BrWRKY65-GFP fusion protein was transiently expressed in Nicotiana benthamiana epidermal cells. PMC12319013.p057.s014: Confocal microscopy showed nuclear-specific GFP fluorescence, co-localizing with DAPI-stained nuclei, while free GFP displayed diffuse cytoplasmic and nuclear distribution. PMC12319013.p057.s015: These results confirm BrWRKY65’s nuclear localization, consistent with our investigation.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression increases antioxidant enzyme activities under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s003: Meanwhile, oxidative markers like MDA, H2O2, and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) serve as indicators of oxidative stress levels. PMC12319013.p059.s004: A precise equilibrium between ROS generation and antioxidant activity is vital; however, heat stress can disrupt this balance, leading to excessive ROS accumulation and subsequent oxidative damage. PMC12319013.p059.s005: Furthermore, Osmolytes, such as proline, serve as key signaling molecules in enhancing plant heat stress tolerance. PMC12319013.p059.s006: Our study revealed that under heat stress (30°C and 35°C), StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants (cultivars Atlantic and Desiree) exhibited increased antioxidant enzyme activity (APX, POD, CAT, SOD), higher proline and chlorophyll levels, and reduced oxidative damage (lower MDA and H2O2) compared to NT plants ( Figures 6 , 7A–F ). PMC12319013.p059.s007: In contrast, StWRKY65 RNAi lines showed the opposite trend.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression upregulates antioxidant genes under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s009: Our findings under heat stress showed similar research trend to a previous research under cold and drought stress, which described that the overexpression of the Malus baccata WRKY TF, MbWRKY65 increased drought and freezing tolerance in transgenic tomatoes by reducing oxidative stress markers (MDA, H2O2, O2 -) and enhancing proline, chlorophyll, and antioxidant enzyme activity. PMC12319013.p059.s010: Additionally, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes (StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2) under heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p059.s011: Conversely, RNAi-mediated suppression of StWRKY65 resulted in downregulation of these genes compared to NT controls ( Figures 8A–T ). PMC12319013.p059.s012: These findings suggest that StWRKY65 plays a pivotal role in modulating antioxidant-related gene expression during heat stress. PMC12319013.p059.s013: A prior study exhibited the functional analysis of TaWRKY31, which revealed contrasting expression patterns of antioxidant genes under drought stress conditions.

events.event_label: High temperature stress upregulates StWRKY65 expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p034.s001: The expression profiles of StWRKY65 under high-temperature stress demonstrated robust and rapid induction ( Figures 2E–H ). PMC12319013.p034.s002: In ‘Desiree’, 30°C treatment caused differential upregulation (gradual increases at 1, 3, and 6 h, a decline at 12 h, followed by rises at 24 and 48 h, peaking at 48 h), whereas 35°C elicited stronger upregulation throughout the time course (particularly from 6 to 48 h), with a rapid response peaking at 6 h ( Figures 2E–G ). PMC12319013.p034.s003: This suggests that StWRKY65 is temperature-responsive and may play a critical role in thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p034.s004: In contrast, ‘Atlantic’ displayed distinct dynamics: at 30°C, rapid transcriptional induction occurred at 1 and 3 h, followed by declines at 6 and 12 h, then gradual increases to a peak at 48 h; under 35°C, sustained upregulation was observed at 1, 3, 6, and 12 h, a decline at 24 h, and a peak at 48 h ( Figures 2F–H ).

events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p042.s003: When subjected to 30°C heat stress, all growth indices of ‘Atlantic’ genotypes were growth-inhibited, but StWRKY65 overexpression lines exhibited stronger adaptability: compared with NT controls, plant height increased by 26.52%–33.45%, plant fresh weight by 22.86%–27.55%, dry weight by 25.38%–30.41%, root fresh weight by 24.07%–32.33%, root dry weight by 26.29%, 36.85%, and tuber weight by 35.59%–47.64%. PMC12319013.p042.s005: Under severe 35°C heat stress, the thermoprotective effect of StWRKY65 in ‘Atlantic’ became more pronounced: OE lines displayed remarkable stress resistance, with plant height increased by 51.10%–64.90%, plant fresh and dry weights by 49.38%–57.67% and 36.11%–36.93%, respectively, root fresh and dry weights by 46.42%–63.97% and 43.15%–57.38%, respectively, and tuber weight by 77.56%–97.53% relative to NT controls. PMC12319013.p042.s006: Conversely, RNAi lines were highly sensitive to heat stress, showing drastic reductions in plant height (51.38%–61.70%), plant fresh and dry weights (57.77%–65.16% and 50.61%–59.24%), root fresh and dry weights (46.53%–56.27% and 43.28%–57.82%), and tuber weight (92.32%–94.91%) ( Figures 4A–F ), compared to NT lines. events.event_label: BaWRKY22 activates and enhances BaCAT2 expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s015: Conversely, heterologous overexpression of TaWRKY31 in Arabidopsis thaliana enhanced transcript levels of orthologous antioxidant genes (AtSOD (Cu/Zn), AtPOD, and AtCAT), which are consistent with our heat stress findings; these results suggest a universal stress adaptation role. PMC12319013.p059.s016: Moreover, in Chinese cabbage, BaWRKY22 activated and enhanced the BaCAT2 transcript level and reduced H2O2 contents under heat stress, and overexpression of the PtWRKY2 gene up-regulated the POD34, CAT1, and SOD1 genes in transgenic Arabidopsis under heat stress. PMC12319013.p059.s017: As a transcription factor, StWRKY65 may directly or indirectly regulate these antioxidant genes or other regulatory factors, thereby increasing the expression of corresponding proteins and enzyme activity. PMC12319013.p059.s018: This enhances the efficient scavenging of ROS (evidenced by reduced H2O2 and MDA levels), protects cellular structures (maintaining membrane stability and photosynthetic pigments), and ultimately promotes growth and yield formation under heat stress.

events.event_label: ZmWRKY65 contains a WRKYGQK domain and a C2H2 zinc-finger motif
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p056.s003: Here, we investigated the function of StWRKY65 under different heat stresses through transgenic techniques to elucidate its regulatory role in thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p056.s004: In this study, we initially investigated the sequence alignment analysis, which revealed that StWRKY65 contains a canonical WRKYGQK domain at its N-terminus, along with a C2H2-type zinc-finger motif (CX5-C-X23-H-X1-H), consistent with its classification as a Group II WRKY transcription factor ( Figure 1A ). PMC12319013.p056.s005: Phylogenetic tree construction further supported this classification, grouping StWRKY65 with functionally related homologs from other plant species, suggesting evolutionary conservation of its regulatory role ( Figure 1B ). PMC12319013.p056.s006: Our investigation is supported by a previous report, which explored that ZmWRKY65, a candidate WRKY TF identified from drought and SA-treated maize transcriptomic data, encodes a 516-amino acid protein featuring a conserved WRKYGQK domain, an N-terminal coiled-coil region (aa 102–142), and a C2H2 zinc-finger motif (CX5CX23 HX1H). PMC12319013.p056.s007: Phylogenetic analysis (MEGA 5.1, neighbor-joining) of 15 homologs from diverse species and 14 maize WRKYs (Phytozome 12.1) revealed close evolutionary relationships between ZmWRKY65 and AtWRKY3 (Arabidopsis), OsWRKY96 (rice), SiWRKY3 (millet), and GmWRKY33 (soybean), suggesting functional conservation among them.

events.event_label: Heat stress reduces photosynthetic efficiency
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p060.s001: Extreme heat stress disrupts the photosynthetic apparatus in plant cells, leading to a significant decline in photosynthetic efficiency. PMC12319013.p060.s002: Our findings reveal that sustained exposure to elevated temperatures (30–35°C) induced significant photosynthetic impairment in both RNAi and NT potato plants. PMC12319013.p060.s003: This thermal stress disrupted photosynthetic performance through a cascade of interconnected physiological distresses, including a marked decline in photosynthetic efficiency, suppression of transpiration rates, and pronounced stomatal dysfunction, as evidenced by aberrant stomatal conductance and aperture regulation. PMC12319013.p060.s004: On the other hand, StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants in both cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’) under varying heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C) exhibited significantly higher photosynthetic rate, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance ( Figures 9A–F ), which maintained stomatal aperture, preserve chloroplast functions, improve water moment, and preserve ROS damage.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 enhances thermotolerance under heat stress
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s006: Additionally, we generated transgenic potato lines with overexpression (OE) or RNA interference (RNAi)-mediated suppression of the target gene. PMC12319013.p002.s007: Under heat stress, OE lines exhibited enhanced thermotolerance, manifested through improvements in plant height, fresh/dry weights of plants and roots, tuber yield, photosynthetic efficiency, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance. PMC12319013.p002.s008: Concurrently, compared to non-transgenic (NT) controls, StWRKY65 overexpression significantly elevated the activities of antioxidant enzymes (ascorbate peroxidase [APX], catalase [CAT], peroxidase [POD], superoxide dismutase [SOD]), proline accumulation, and total chlorophyll content, while reducing malondialdehyde (MDA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) levels. PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p002.s010: Furthermore, StWRKY65 overexpression transcriptionally activated key antioxidant enzyme-related genes (StAPX, StCAT1/2, StPOD12/47, StFeSOD2/3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1/2), whereas its knockdown produced opposing effects.

events.event_label: StWRKY65‑GFP fusion localizes to nucleus
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p037.s001: To elucidate the subcellular localization of StWRKY65, its coding sequence was cloned into the pCAM35S expression vector carrying the green fluorescent protein (GFP) reporter gene. PMC12319013.p037.s002: The constructed StWRKY65-GFP fusion vector, along with the empty GFP vector (control), was transiently expressed in epidermal cells of Nicotiana benthamiana via Agrobacterium tumefaciens (strain GV3101)-mediated transformation. PMC12319013.p037.s003: Confocal laser scanning microscopy analysis revealed distinct subcellular distribution patterns: The free GFP control exhibited fluorescence in the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus, indicating its nonspecific cellular localization. PMC12319013.p037.s004: In contrast, the StWRKY65-GFP fusion protein displayed strong green fluorescence signals exclusively in the nuclear region, with no detectable fluorescence in the cytoplasm or plasma membrane ( Figure 3 ). PMC12319013.p037.s005: This strict nuclear localization pattern confirms that StWRKY65 specifically resides in the nucleus, a key characteristic of functional TFs involved in transcriptional regulation. PMC12319013.p037.s006: These findings provide crucial structural evidence supporting the presumed role of StWRKY65 in regulating gene expression networks within the nucleus, laying a foundation for further mechanistic studies on its biological functions.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 confers thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p058.s001: Abiotic stress, such as extreme temperatures, disrupts plants’ key physiological and biochemical processes, ultimately hindering growth and biomass production. PMC12319013.p058.s002: To counteract these challenges, plants activate sophisticated defense mechanisms or key genes, which modulate growth patterns and restore metabolic pathways to maintain cellular homeostasis and ensure survival. PMC12319013.p058.s003: Our study investigated the role of the StWRKY65 gene in enhancing thermotolerance in potato plants under heat stress (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p058.s004: Remarkably, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited a significant increase in plant height, tuber yield, total fresh and dry biomass, and root systems compared to NT controls, as recorded in Figures 4 , 5A–F . PMC12319013.p058.s005: Conversely, RNAi-silenced plants showed a decline in these growth parameters, confirming the gene’s critical role in heat stress resilience. PMC12319013.p058.s006: Notably, under optimal temperatures, all experimental plants performed equally, supporting that StWRKY65 specifically functions in thermotolerance rather than baseline growth. PMC12319013.p058.s007: In a previous report, after mannitol (200–300 mM) stress application, lateral root number remained unchanged, but root length decreased in WT and OE lines.

events.event_label: BaWRKY22 upregulates BaCAT2 and reduces H2O2
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s015: Conversely, heterologous overexpression of TaWRKY31 in Arabidopsis thaliana enhanced transcript levels of orthologous antioxidant genes (AtSOD (Cu/Zn), AtPOD, and AtCAT), which are consistent with our heat stress findings; these results suggest a universal stress adaptation role. PMC12319013.p059.s016: Moreover, in Chinese cabbage, BaWRKY22 activated and enhanced the BaCAT2 transcript level and reduced H2O2 contents under heat stress, and overexpression of the PtWRKY2 gene up-regulated the POD34, CAT1, and SOD1 genes in transgenic Arabidopsis under heat stress. PMC12319013.p059.s017: As a transcription factor, StWRKY65 may directly or indirectly regulate these antioxidant genes or other regulatory factors, thereby increasing the expression of corresponding proteins and enzyme activity. PMC12319013.p059.s018: This enhances the efficient scavenging of ROS (evidenced by reduced H2O2 and MDA levels), protects cellular structures (maintaining membrane stability and photosynthetic pigments), and ultimately promotes growth and yield formation under heat stress.

events.event_label: PtWRKY2 upregulates POD34, CAT1 and SOD1

PMC12319013.p059.s016: Moreover, in Chinese cabbage, BaWRKY22 activated and enhanced the BaCAT2 transcript level and reduced H2O2 contents under heat stress, and overexpression of the PtWRKY2 gene up-regulated the POD34, CAT1, and SOD1 genes in transgenic Arabidopsis under heat stress. events.event_label: StWRKY65 modulates photosynthetic parameters and confers heat tolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p053.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, StWRKY65-overexpressing lines of both potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) compared to NT plants, whereas RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant decreases in these gas exchange parameters. PMC12319013.p053.s004: Similarly, under 35°C heat stress, all experimental plants of both cultivars experienced a reduction in photosynthetic efficiency, but StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) still maintained significantly higher Pn ( Figures 9A, B ), Gs ( Figures 9C–E ), ( Figures 9E, F ) than NT lines, while RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed further decreases in these photosynthetic parameters. PMC12319013.p053.s005: The contrasting responses between overexpression and knockout genotypes strongly support the regulatory role of StWRKY65 in maintaining photosynthetic efficiency under high-temperature stress (30°C and 35°C).

events.event_label: StWRKY65 increases proline and chlorophyll, decreases MDA and H2O2
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s006: Additionally, we generated transgenic potato lines with overexpression (OE) or RNA interference (RNAi)-mediated suppression of the target gene. PMC12319013.p002.s007: Under heat stress, OE lines exhibited enhanced thermotolerance, manifested through improvements in plant height, fresh/dry weights of plants and roots, tuber yield, photosynthetic efficiency, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance. PMC12319013.p002.s008: Concurrently, compared to non-transgenic (NT) controls, StWRKY65 overexpression significantly elevated the activities of antioxidant enzymes (ascorbate peroxidase [APX], catalase [CAT], peroxidase [POD], superoxide dismutase [SOD]), proline accumulation, and total chlorophyll content, while reducing malondialdehyde (MDA) and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) levels. PMC12319013.p002.s009: Conversely, RNAi lines displayed heightened heat sensitivity, impaired growth parameters, diminished antioxidant capacity, and elevated oxidative stress markers. PMC12319013.p002.s010: Furthermore, StWRKY65 overexpression transcriptionally activated key antioxidant enzyme-related genes (StAPX, StCAT1/2, StPOD12/47, StFeSOD2/3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1/2), whereas its knockdown produced opposing effects.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 overexpression increases activities of APX, CAT, POD and SOD
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p063.s001: This research highlights the essential function of StWRKY65, a Group II WRKY transcription factor localized in the nucleus, in improving thermotolerance in potato. PMC12319013.p063.s002: Overexpression of StWRKY65 markedly improves thermotolerance by enhancing the activities of key antioxidant enzymes (APX, CAT, POD, SOD), increasing proline levels, maintain chlorophyll content, optimizing photosynthetic parameters (including photosynthesis, transpiration, and stomatal conductance), and decreasing H2O2 and MDA accumulation. PMC12319013.p063.s003: Additionally, the overexpression of StWRKY65 was shown to transcriptionally activate important genes associated with antioxidant enzymes, including StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2. PMC12319013.p063.s004: In contrast, plants with StWRKY65 knockdown show reduced thermotolerance and adverse physiological responses.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s003: Meanwhile, oxidative markers like MDA, H2O2, and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) serve as indicators of oxidative stress levels. events.event_label: high temperature regulates WRKY33 and WRKY25
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p005.s009: Some WRKY proteins also feature additional domains, such as glutamate/proline-rich regions and leucine zippers, which may influence function. PMC12319013.p005.s010: Previous studies demonstrated that the WRKY TFs play key roles in plant abiotic stress responses, such as in Arabidopsis thaliana, high temperature suppresses AtWRKY33 but induces AtWRKY25/26, and their overexpression enhances heat resistance. PMC12319013.p005.s011: Similarly, AtWRKY39 (Group II) is activated by heat and regulated by SA/JA signaling pathways, with its overexpression improving thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p005.s012: In tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum), NtWRKY65 regulates the expression of genes related to salt stress. PMC12319013.p005.s013: In wheat (Triticum aestivum), TaWRKY33 overexpression improves heat tolerance, demonstrating WRKY TFs’ diverse roles in stress responses.

events.event_label: PEG6000 induces StWRKY65 expression and is associated with drought
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p032.s001: This study systematically analyzed the expression profiles of the StWRKY65 gene in leaves of two potato cultivars (‘Desiree’ and ‘Atlantic’) under various abiotic stress conditions ( Figures 2A–L ). PMC12319013.p032.s002: Under drought stress, StWRKY65 exhibited expression patterns correlated with stress severity and cultivar specificity. PMC12319013.p032.s003: In ‘Desiree’, 10% PEG6000 treatment induced a stable upregulation (with relatively steady increases at 1, 3, 6, 12, and 48 h post-treatment, peaking at 6 h), whereas 20% PEG6000 triggered fluctuating upregulation (rapid induction at 1 h, followed by a decline at 3 h, then gradual increases at 6, 12, and 24 h, reaching a peak at 24 h) ( Figures 2A–C ), suggesting its positive regulatory role under severe drought. PMC12319013.p032.s004: In ‘Atlantic’, 10% PEG6000 elicited oscillatory expression (upregulation at 1, 3, 24, and 48 h but downregulation at 6 and 12 h), while 20% PEG6000 induced variable upregulation (peaking at 12 h) ( Figures 2B–D ), indicating its potential involvement in drought resistance.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 and GFP localize to nucleus
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p057.s009: As TFs, WRKY proteins are predominantly localized in the nucleus and exhibit transcriptional regulatory activity. PMC12319013.p057.s010: Subcellular localization analysis demonstrated nuclear localization of the StWRKY65 protein, as evidenced by distinct GFP fluorescence signals exclusively detected in the nucleus ( Figure 3 ). PMC12319013.p057.s011: In contrast, control vectors expressing free GFP exhibited diffuse fluorescence patterns distributed across the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nuclear compartments. PMC12319013.p057.s012: This observation confirms the nuclear targeting capability of StWRKY65, consistent with its predicted function as a TF. PMC12319013.p057.s013: The BrWRKY65-GFP fusion protein was transiently expressed in Nicotiana benthamiana epidermal cells.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 positively regulates thermotolerance; knockdown impairs it
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p063.s001: This research highlights the essential function of StWRKY65, a Group II WRKY transcription factor localized in the nucleus, in improving thermotolerance in potato. PMC12319013.p063.s002: Overexpression of StWRKY65 markedly improves thermotolerance by enhancing the activities of key antioxidant enzymes (APX, CAT, POD, SOD), increasing proline levels, maintain chlorophyll content, optimizing photosynthetic parameters (including photosynthesis, transpiration, and stomatal conductance), and decreasing H2O2 and MDA accumulation. PMC12319013.p063.s003: Additionally, the overexpression of StWRKY65 was shown to transcriptionally activate important genes associated with antioxidant enzymes, including StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2. PMC12319013.p063.s004: In contrast, plants with StWRKY65 knockdown show reduced thermotolerance and adverse physiological responses. PMC12319013.p063.s005: These results position StWRKY65 as a promising target for molecular breeding aimed at enhancing heat resilience in potato and related species, thereby providing a sustainable approach to address the challenges posed by climate change on agricultural productivity.

PMC12319013.p057.s004: qRT-PCR-based expression profiling revealed cultivar-specific differential regulation of StWRKY65 under heat stress (30°C and 35°C) at multiple time points. PMC12319013.p057.s012: This observation confirms the nuclear targeting capability of StWRKY65, consistent with its predicted function as a TF. events.event_label: heat stress enhances CAT and SOD activities
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p046.s001: To investigate the physiological responses of transgenic and NT potato lines of the cultivars ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ under different temperature stress conditions (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C), the key antioxidant enzymes (APX, CAT, POD, SOD), oxidative stress marker (H2O2 and MDA), osmoprotectant proline, and total chlorophyll content were compared between NT and transgenic lines (OE and RNAi), as shown in Figures 6 , 7 . PMC12319013.p046.s002: Under the control condition (20°C), no significant differences (P > 0.05) were observed in all the aforementioned physiological indicators between transgenic and NT plants. PMC12319013.p046.s003: Under the conditions of 30°C and 35°C, the activities of APX ( Figures 6 , 7A ), CAT ( Figures 6 , 7B ), POD ( Figures 6 , 7C ), and SOD ( Figures 6 , 7D ) in the OE lines were significantly higher than those in the NT and RNAi lines (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001), indicating that the overexpression of StWRKY65 could enhance the antioxidant defense system and more efficiently scavenge reactive oxygen species (ROS) under heat stress. PMC12319013.p046.s004: Meanwhile, the contents of oxidative stress markers H2O2 ( Figures 6 , 7E ) and MDA ( Figures 6 , 7F ) were also analyzed.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 enhances thermotolerance and biomass accumulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p058.s001: Abiotic stress, such as extreme temperatures, disrupts plants’ key physiological and biochemical processes, ultimately hindering growth and biomass production. PMC12319013.p058.s002: To counteract these challenges, plants activate sophisticated defense mechanisms or key genes, which modulate growth patterns and restore metabolic pathways to maintain cellular homeostasis and ensure survival. PMC12319013.p058.s003: Our study investigated the role of the StWRKY65 gene in enhancing thermotolerance in potato plants under heat stress (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p058.s004: Remarkably, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited a significant increase in plant height, tuber yield, total fresh and dry biomass, and root systems compared to NT controls, as recorded in Figures 4 , 5A–F . PMC12319013.p058.s005: Conversely, RNAi-silenced plants showed a decline in these growth parameters, confirming the gene’s critical role in heat stress resilience. PMC12319013.p058.s006: Notably, under optimal temperatures, all experimental plants performed equally, supporting that StWRKY65 specifically functions in thermotolerance rather than baseline growth. PMC12319013.p058.s007: In a previous report, after mannitol (200–300 mM) stress application, lateral root number remained unchanged, but root length decreased in WT and OE lines.

events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s003: Meanwhile, oxidative markers like MDA, H2O2, and reactive nitrogen species (RNS) serve as indicators of oxidative stress levels. PMC12319013.p059.s004: A precise equilibrium between ROS generation and antioxidant activity is vital; however, heat stress can disrupt this balance, leading to excessive ROS accumulation and subsequent oxidative damage. PMC12319013.p059.s005: Furthermore, Osmolytes, such as proline, serve as key signaling molecules in enhancing plant heat stress tolerance. PMC12319013.p059.s006: Our study revealed that under heat stress (30°C and 35°C), StWRKY65-overexpressing potato plants (cultivars Atlantic and Desiree) exhibited increased antioxidant enzyme activity (APX, POD, CAT, SOD), higher proline and chlorophyll levels, and reduced oxidative damage (lower MDA and H2O2) compared to NT plants ( Figures 6 , 7A–F ). PMC12319013.p059.s007: In contrast, StWRKY65 RNAi lines showed the opposite trend.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 regulates StCAT1 and StPOD47 expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p051.s001: StWRKY65 regulates the relative mRNA expression of potato plants of cultivar ‘Atlantic’ ‘Desiree’; (A, B) StAPX, (C, D) StCAT1, (E, F) StCAT2, (G, H) StPOD12, (I, J) StPOD47, (K, L) StFeSOD2, (M, N) StFeSOD3, (O, P) StMnSOD, (Q, R) StCuZnSOD1, and (S, T) StCuZnSOD2, respectively, after exposure to 20°C, 30°C, and 35°C of heat stress. PMC12319013.p051.s002: In the ‘Atlantic’ cultivar: NT: non-transgenic plants; OE, pBI121-EGFP-StWRKY65-transgenic plants (OE-1, OE-2, and OE-4); RNAi, pART-StWRKY65-RNAi-transgenic plants (RNAi-1, RNAi-3, and RNAi-5). PMC12319013.p051.s003: In the ‘Desiree’ cultivar: NT: non-transgenic plants; OE, pBI121-EGFP-StWRKY65-transgenic plants (OE-1, OE-3, and OE-4); RNAi, pART-StWRKY65-RNAi-transgenic plants (RNAi-2, RNAi-3, and RNAi-5). PMC12319013.p051.s004: The data are presented as mean ± standard deviation.

events.event_label: RNAi of StWRKY65 decreases StCAT1 and StCuZnSOD2 expression
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p059.s009: Our findings under heat stress showed similar research trend to a previous research under cold and drought stress, which described that the overexpression of the Malus baccata WRKY TF, MbWRKY65 increased drought and freezing tolerance in transgenic tomatoes by reducing oxidative stress markers (MDA, H2O2, O2 -) and enhancing proline, chlorophyll, and antioxidant enzyme activity. PMC12319013.p059.s010: Additionally, StWRKY65-overexpressing plants exhibited significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes (StAPX, StCAT1, StCAT2, StPOD12, StPOD47, StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3, StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2) under heat stress conditions (30°C and 35°C). PMC12319013.p059.s011: Conversely, RNAi-mediated suppression of StWRKY65 resulted in downregulation of these genes compared to NT controls ( Figures 8A–T ). PMC12319013.p059.s012: These findings suggest that StWRKY65 plays a pivotal role in modulating antioxidant-related gene expression during heat stress. PMC12319013.p059.s013: A prior study exhibited the functional analysis of TaWRKY31, which revealed contrasting expression patterns of antioxidant genes under drought stress conditions.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 activates_or_induces thermotolerance
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p001.s001: StWRKY65 stimulates thermotolerance in potato (Solanum tuberosum L.) through antioxidant and photosynthetic modulation

events.event_label: Heat stress reduces_or_impairs growth
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s001: Heat stress severely impacts the growth and development of potato plants. PMC12319013.p002.s002: However, the molecular mechanisms underlying thermotolerance, particularly the role of WRKY transcription factors (TFs), remain poorly understood. PMC12319013.p002.s003: Here, we identified StWRKY65 as a heat-responsive gene in potato, demonstrating significant transcriptional upregulation under 30°C and 35°C heat stress conditions. PMC12319013.p002.s004: Phylogenetic analysis classified StWRKY65 into the WRKY Group II family, revealing high evolutionary conservation with its tomato ortholog SlWRKY65.

events.event_label: Heat stress increases_expression_of StWRKY65
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s001: Heat stress severely impacts the growth and development of potato plants. PMC12319013.p002.s002: However, the molecular mechanisms underlying thermotolerance, particularly the role of WRKY transcription factors (TFs), remain poorly understood. PMC12319013.p002.s003: Here, we identified StWRKY65 as a heat-responsive gene in potato, demonstrating significant transcriptional upregulation under 30°C and 35°C heat stress conditions. PMC12319013.p002.s004: Phylogenetic analysis classified StWRKY65 into the WRKY Group II family, revealing high evolutionary conservation with its tomato ortholog SlWRKY65.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 localizes_to nuclear
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p002.s003: Here, we identified StWRKY65 as a heat-responsive gene in potato, demonstrating significant transcriptional upregulation under 30°C and 35°C heat stress conditions. PMC12319013.p002.s004: Phylogenetic analysis classified StWRKY65 into the WRKY Group II family, revealing high evolutionary conservation with its tomato ortholog SlWRKY65. PMC12319013.p002.s005: Subcellular localization confirmed its nuclear targeting. PMC12319013.p002.s006: Additionally, we generated transgenic potato lines with overexpression (OE) or RNA interference (RNAi)-mediated suppression of the target gene. PMC12319013.p002.s007: Under heat stress, OE lines exhibited enhanced thermotolerance, manifested through improvements in plant height, fresh/dry weights of plants and roots, tuber yield, photosynthetic efficiency, transpiration rate, and stomatal conductance.

events.event_label: heat activates_or_induces AtWRKY39
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p005.s009: Some WRKY proteins also feature additional domains, such as glutamate/proline-rich regions and leucine zippers, which may influence function. PMC12319013.p005.s010: Previous studies demonstrated that the WRKY TFs play key roles in plant abiotic stress responses, such as in Arabidopsis thaliana, high temperature suppresses AtWRKY33 but induces AtWRKY25/26, and their overexpression enhances heat resistance. PMC12319013.p005.s011: Similarly, AtWRKY39 (Group II) is activated by heat and regulated by SA/JA signaling pathways, with its overexpression improving thermotolerance. PMC12319013.p005.s012: In tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum), NtWRKY65 regulates the expression of genes related to salt stress. PMC12319013.p005.s013: In wheat (Triticum aestivum), TaWRKY33 overexpression improves heat tolerance, demonstrating WRKY TFs’ diverse roles in stress responses.

PMC12319013.p005.s011: Similarly, AtWRKY39 (Group II) is activated by heat and regulated by SA/JA signaling pathways, with its overexpression improving thermotolerance. events.event_label: WRKY is_detected_in potato
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p006.s001: Despite the identification of 79 WRKY genes in potato and the characterization of several members (e.g., StWRKY19/25/29) in abiotic stress responses, the specific role of StWRKY65 in heat stress tolerance remains completely unknown. PMC12319013.p006.s002: This represents a critical knowledge gap because WRKY TFs are master regulators of plant stress responses, yet potato WRKY networks are poorly understood. PMC12319013.p006.s003: Heat stress is becoming increasingly detrimental to potato yields under climate change, and preliminary evidence suggests StWRKY65 may have unique functional properties distinct from other stress-responsive WRKYs. PMC12319013.p006.s004: Our study is the first to systematically investigate StWRKY65’s involvement in heat stress adaptation, which could reveal novel mechanisms of thermotolerance in this globally important crop.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 is_expressed_in leaves
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p033.s001: The expression patterns of StWRKY65 in the leaves of potato cultivar ‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’ in response to drought (10% and 20% PEG6000), heat (30°C and 35°C), and salinity (75 mM and 150 mM NaCl), induced stress at various time intervals (0, 1, 3, 6, 12, 24, and 48 h). PMC12319013.p033.s002: The data are presented as mean ± standard deviation. PMC12319013.p033.s003: P-values (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001) were calculated through ordinary two-way ANOVA followed by Tukey’s multiple comparisons test with a sample size of n = 9.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 participates_in transcriptional regulation
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p037.s003: Confocal laser scanning microscopy analysis revealed distinct subcellular distribution patterns: The free GFP control exhibited fluorescence in the plasma membrane, cytoplasm, and nucleus, indicating its nonspecific cellular localization. PMC12319013.p037.s004: In contrast, the StWRKY65-GFP fusion protein displayed strong green fluorescence signals exclusively in the nuclear region, with no detectable fluorescence in the cytoplasm or plasma membrane ( Figure 3 ). PMC12319013.p037.s005: This strict nuclear localization pattern confirms that StWRKY65 specifically resides in the nucleus, a key characteristic of functional TFs involved in transcriptional regulation. PMC12319013.p037.s006: These findings provide crucial structural evidence supporting the presumed role of StWRKY65 in regulating gene expression networks within the nucleus, laying a foundation for further mechanistic studies on its biological functions.

events.event_label: StWRKY65 increases_expression_of StFeSOD3
events.evidence_context_text: PMC12319013.p050.s001: To further elucidate the role of StWRKY65 in response to varying temperature stress conditions (20°C, 30°C, and 35°C), we analyzed the expression of stress-responsive genes in two potato cultivars (‘Atlantic’ and ‘Desiree’), including StAPX, StCAT1,​StCAT2, tPOD12, StPOD47 StFeSOD2, StFeSOD3,​StMnSOD, StCuZnSOD1, and StCuZnSOD2. PMC12319013.p050.s002: As shown in Figures 8A–T , under 20°C, no significant differences in the relative mRNA expression levels of these antioxidant-related genes were observed between transgenic (OE and RNAi) and NT plants in either cultivar. PMC12319013.p050.s003: Under 30°C heat stress, the StWRKY65-overexpressing lines (‘Atlantic’: OE-1/2/4; ‘Desiree’: OE-1/3/4) exhibited​significant upregulation of stress-responsive genes, including StAPX ( Figures 8A, B ), StCAT1 ( Figures 8C, D ) StCAT2 ( Figures 8E, F ), StPOD12 ( Figures 8G, H ), StPOD47 ( Figures 8I, J ), StFeSOD2 ( Figures 8K, L ), StFeSOD3 ( Figures 8M, N ), StMnSOD ( Figures 8O, P ), StCuZnSOD1 ( Figures 8Q, R ), and StCuZnSOD2 ( Figures 8S, T ), compared to NT controls. PMC12319013.p050.s004: In contrast, the RNAi lines (‘Atlantic’: RNAi-1/3/4; ‘Desiree’: RNAi-2/3/5) showed significant downregulation of these genes under the same stress conditions.
